stories.title: Lovlina Borgohain locks in India's first Commonwealth Games 2026 medal — without throwing a punch
key_points: Lovlina gets a bye straight into the women's 75kg boxing semifinal
Commonwealth Games boxing awards bronze to both losing semifinalists
That bye alone guarantees her a medal — India's first confirmed medal of these Games
Her semifinal is July 31, against Tuvalu's Tarona Taafaki
stories.background: Lovlina Borgohain, a Tokyo Olympics bronze medallist, carried India's flag into the Commonwealth Games 2026 opening ceremony in Glasgow this week. The women's 75kg boxing category drew only five entrants this year, which changes how the draw works: instead of a full bracket of quarterfinals, some fighters skip straight to the semis.
stories.what_happened: Because only five boxers entered the women's 75kg category, only one quarterfinal (England's Mary Kate Smith vs Nigeria's Patricia Mbata, on July 28) is needed. Lovlina, along with Australia's Emma-Sue Greentree and Tuvalu's Tarona Taafaki, advanced directly into the semifinals without fighting. Since the Commonwealth Games gives out two bronze medals per boxing weight class — one to each losing semifinalist — simply reaching the semifinal guarantees Lovlina at least a bronze.
stories.why_it_matters: It's India's first confirmed medal at these Games, a psychological boost for the wider contingent, and it guarantees a podium finish for one of India's most recognizable Olympic boxers even before she has thrown a competitive punch here.
stories.ripple_effects: It adds an early medal to India's tally, which matters for the overall medal-table narrative other athletes and the Indian media will be tracking through the rest of the Games. It also means Lovlina now has a real shot at gold or silver, since she only needs to win once more (the semifinal) to reach the final.
stories.why_now: The knockout draw for the 75kg category was only finalized this week once the Games got underway and the full entry list for boxing was set.
stories.watch_next: Watch her July 31 semifinal against Tarona Taafaki — win it, and she's guaranteed at least silver.

key_terms.definition: When a competitor automatically advances to the next round without playing an opponent, usually because the draw doesn't divide evenly.

stories.the_lesson: In knockout sports, the size of the field — not just how good you are — can change your medal odds; a shallow entry list can hand a guaranteed medal to someone who hasn't even competed yet.

stories.title: India dodges the higher tariff bracket as the old US tariff deal expires — lands 10%, not 12.5%
key_points: The temporary 10% US tariff on Indian goods expired at 9:31am IST today
Washington's new tariff slabs put India in the lower 10% bracket, not the initially-planned 12.5%
About 92% of India's $87.2 billion in US-bound exports return to normal WTO tariff rates
A full India-US trade deal is still NOT signed — this is just today's tariff reset
stories.background: For months, India and the US have been racing to finalize a bilateral trade agreement before a temporary US tariff arrangement expired. As of yesterday, negotiators were still stuck on the 'last one percent' of the legal text — sticking points included how much of a tariff edge India gets over rivals like Vietnam, and US demands for guarantees that Indian supply chains (textiles, seafood, manufacturing) are free of forced labor.
stories.what_happened: The 10% temporary US tariff on Indian exports expired at 9:31am IST today. Rather than a full signed trade deal, the US instead issued new tariff slabs under its Section 301 trade law, initially expected to put India in a 12.5% bracket — but after last-minute discussions on labor practices, India secured the lower 10% rate instead. That relief applies to roughly 92% of India's $87.2 billion in annual merchandise exports to the US, which return to normal, most-favoured-nation tariff rates. No comprehensive deal text has been signed or published.
stories.why_it_matters: For Indian exporters — especially in textiles, gems and jewelry, and engineering goods — this is the difference between staying price-competitive in the US market and losing ground to competitors like Vietnam and Indonesia. But it's a temporary reprieve, not a resolution: the underlying trade negotiation is still unfinished.
stories.ripple_effects: Export-heavy Indian sectors and their stocks may see near-term relief. But businesses still can't plan long-term around a stable tariff rate, since Washington could revise it again once ongoing US Trade Representative investigations wrap up. It also keeps pressure on Indian and US negotiators to finish the actual bilateral deal rather than rely on rolling temporary arrangements.
stories.why_now: Today, July 24, was the hard deadline written into the original temporary tariff order — so the rate had to reset one way or another today, deal or no deal.
stories.watch_next: Watch for the 'joint statement' commerce ministry officials have suggested may substitute for a fully signed deal — and what tariff rate applies once the pending US Trade Representative investigations conclude.

key_terms.definition: A US trade law that lets Washington impose tariffs on a country unilaterally, often used as leverage in trade negotiations.
The standard, baseline tariff rate a country charges under WTO rules, absent any special deal or dispute-driven surcharge.

stories.the_lesson: In trade negotiations, deadlines rarely produce a clean final deal — more often they produce a temporary patch that avoids the worst outcome while the real negotiation drags on.
stories.market_link: Export-heavy Indian sectors (textiles, gems & jewellery, engineering goods, IT services with US exposure) and the rupee may react; watch Nifty export-linked stocks.

thread.previously: We reported the deal was stuck at the 'last one percent' of legal text, with the tariff deadline just hours away and no resolution in sight.
thread.changed: The deadline has now passed — no full deal was signed, but India secured a better-than-expected outcome: a 10% tariff rate instead of the 12.5% initially floated, sparing most of its US-bound exports.

stories.title: India's wholesale prices post their sharpest jump in over a decade — and traders now bet the RBI has to raise rates
key_points: Wholesale price inflation (WPI) jumped to about 9.87% in June — the fastest pace outside the Covid years
Fuel, power and manufactured goods are the biggest drivers, as oil stays elevated on Middle East tensions
Swap markets now price roughly a 1-in-3 chance the RBI hikes rates as soon as October, up from near 1-in-10 a week ago
The rupee is trading near a record low, around ₹96.5 per dollar
stories.background: WPI (Wholesale Price Index) tracks how much prices change at the factory-gate/wholesale level — before goods reach a shop shelf — and is different from the CPI retail inflation number most people follow, though WPI moves tend to show up in retail prices with a lag. The RBI (Reserve Bank of India) is India's central bank; its main job is keeping inflation in check, mainly by setting the repo rate, the interest rate at which it lends to banks. Until recently, markets expected the RBI to keep cutting rates to support growth.
stories.what_happened: The RBI's own July bulletin described the economy as broadly resilient, even as it flagged rising WPI and elevated global oil prices as risks. Wholesale inflation has climbed to roughly 9.87% for June — its steepest rise outside the pandemic years — while the rupee trades near an all-time low against the dollar. Interest-rate swap markets are now pricing meaningfully higher odds of an RBI rate hike at its October meeting.
stories.why_it_matters: A hike would be a sharp reversal from the rate-cut path markets had been expecting to support growth, which Deloitte already sees slowing to a modest 6.5-6.8% this year. Higher rates make loans costlier for households and businesses right when growth is already cooling.
stories.ripple_effects: Costlier home, auto and business loans if a hike materializes; more pressure on a stock market that's already fallen four straight sessions; a weaker rupee raises the price of everything India imports, especially oil and electronics; the government's own borrowing gets more expensive too.
stories.why_now: The same Iran-Israel and Houthi-driven conflict pushing Brent crude toward $96 a barrel is hitting India twice over — India imports roughly 85% of the oil it uses, so pricier crude both widens its import bill and now feeds directly into rate-hike bets.
stories.watch_next: The RBI's Monetary Policy Committee's next scheduled decision, and whether June's separate CPI retail-inflation print confirms the same upward pressure WPI is showing.

key_terms.definition: An index tracking price changes at the wholesale, factory-gate level — a leading indicator that often shows up in retail prices later.
The interest rate at which the RBI lends short-term money to commercial banks — its main tool for controlling inflation; raising it makes borrowing costlier across the whole economy.

stories.the_lesson: Oil isn't just a commodity story for India — as a country that imports most of what it burns, a price spike abroad shows up everywhere at home: at the fuel pump, in the currency, and eventually in the interest rate you pay on a loan.
stories.market_link: A real rate-hike risk from the RBI, layered on a rupee near record lows, is a headwind for both Indian bonds and equities — and is a direct driver behind the Sensex and Nifty's four-day slide.

stories.title: Adani Group weighs launching its own airline — nudged by a government frustrated with Air India and IndiGo
key_points: Adani Group is evaluating a new airline, or buying into an existing one
The government has privately nudged big business groups to consider entering aviation
Comes after scrutiny of Air India post-crash and IndiGo's December disruptions
Current rules cap airport operators like Adani at 10% ownership in any airline
stories.background: Adani already runs eight Indian airports — including large stakes in Mumbai's — through its ports-to-infrastructure conglomerate, but had previously said it wasn't looking to enter the airline business itself. India's aviation market is dominated by just two carriers, IndiGo and Air India, both of which have had a rough stretch: Air India has been under scrutiny since last year's crash, and IndiGo was hit by major operational meltdowns in December.
stories.what_happened: Sources say Adani Group is now evaluating launching a new airline, or alternatively investing in an existing carrier, after the Indian government privately encouraged large business houses to consider entering the sector. Because current rules cap an airport operator's stake in any airline at 10%, any serious Adani entry would need a regulatory waiver, requiring sign-off from the law ministry and the federal Cabinet. No final decision has been announced.
stories.why_it_matters: A serious Adani entry would be the biggest shake-up to India's airline duopoly in years, potentially easing the market-concentration risk that made Indian air travel fragile to any single carrier's problems — as December's IndiGo disruptions showed.
stories.ripple_effects: Could pressure IndiGo and Air India competitively on pricing and reliability; raises conflict-of-interest questions since Adani would then own both airports and an airline it operates from them; likely to reignite debate over India's cross-ownership aviation rules.
stories.why_now: Follows directly from IndiGo's December operational crisis and continuing Air India safety scrutiny, both of which have made the government uneasy about how concentrated — and fragile — India's airline market has become.
stories.watch_next: Whether the law ministry and Cabinet actually grant a cross-ownership waiver, and whether Adani opts to build a new airline or buy a stake in an existing one.

key_terms.definition: A rule limiting how much of an airline a company that also operates airports can own, meant to prevent one player controlling both the runway and the planes on it.

stories.the_lesson: When a market has only two real players, any shock to either one becomes a national problem — governments often respond to that fragility not by fixing the incumbents, but by trying to create a credible third option.
stories.market_link: Watch Adani Enterprises and Adani-linked airport stocks for signals, and IndiGo/Air India (InterGlobe Aviation) for any competitive-pressure reaction if the plan firms up.

stories.title: Sensex and Nifty fall for a fourth straight day as Gulf tensions push oil prices higher
key_points: Sensex fell 364 points (0.47%) to close at 76,391; Nifty fell 127 points to 23,870
Fourth consecutive losing session for both benchmark indices
Rising crude oil prices, tied to Gulf tensions, are the main driver
Auto stocks and a few heavyweight shares cushioned the fall
stories.background: India imports more than 80% of the crude oil it uses, so its economy and stock market are unusually exposed to Middle East supply shocks. This week's Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers and the broader collapse of the Iran ceasefire have already been rattling global oil markets, and Indian equities import that risk directly through higher fuel costs, inflation pressure, and a weaker rupee.
stories.what_happened: Both benchmark indices dropped for a fourth straight session on Thursday: the Sensex closed down about 364 points at 76,391.39, and the Nifty fell about 127 points to settle at 23,869.60. Rising Brent crude prices — tracking the Yemen-linked Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers and the stalled Iran ceasefire — combined with weak global cues to weigh on sentiment, and broader small- and mid-cap stocks fell roughly 1% as well.
stories.why_it_matters: A sustained four-day losing streak signals investors are pricing in a real, ongoing cost from Middle East instability rather than a one-day scare. Higher oil directly threatens India's import bill, its current-account deficit, and its inflation outlook.
stories.ripple_effects: A weaker rupee and pricier fuel can feed into consumer inflation, complicate the RBI's coming rate decisions, and squeeze margins at oil-import-heavy sectors like aviation, paints, and chemicals — IndiGo's fuel-cost-driven quarterly loss, reported this same week, is a direct example of exactly this channel at work.
stories.why_now: The losing streak comes as this week's Houthi attack on Saudi tankers and the broader collapse of the Iran ceasefire have both intensified, keeping a geopolitical risk premium baked into oil prices.
stories.watch_next: Whether crude keeps climbing if Gulf tensions escalate further, and whether the RBI or government signal any policy response if the losing streak continues.

key_terms.definition: India's two main stock market benchmarks — the Sensex tracks 30 large BSE-listed companies, the Nifty tracks 50 large NSE-listed companies.
The global benchmark price for oil; when it rises, India's oil-import bill and inflation outlook both tend to worsen, since India buys most of its crude from abroad.

stories.the_lesson: India's stock market doesn't just react to Indian news — because the country imports most of its oil, a war half a world away in the Persian Gulf shows up directly in the price of Indian stocks within days.
stories.market_link: Direct — oil-import-sensitive sectors (aviation, paints, tyres) are the most exposed; a falling rupee and rising crude are the transmission channel from Gulf geopolitics to Dalal Street.

stories.title: Indo-MIM, the world's largest metal-injection-molding maker, opens a ₹3,811 crore IPO
key_points: Price band set at ₹461–485 per share, open July 23–27
₹500 crore fresh issue, rest is existing shareholders selling out
Company holds 6.8% of the global MIM component market
Shares expected to list on NSE and BSE around July 30
stories.background: Metal injection molding (MIM) is a manufacturing technique that combines fine metal powder with a binder to mass-produce small, complex, high-precision metal parts — used in everything from surgical tools to consumer electronics to defense components. Indo-MIM has built itself into the largest company in the world doing this, with over 25 years in the business, and is now going public in one of the larger Indian IPOs of the year.
stories.what_happened: Indo-MIM's ₹3,811.21 crore initial public offering opened for subscription on July 23 and runs through July 27, with a price band of ₹461 to ₹485 per share. The offer includes a ₹500 crore fresh issue (new shares, raising new capital for the company) and a much larger ₹3,311.21 crore offer-for-sale, where existing shareholders are cashing out part of their stake. Allotment is expected around July 28, with listing on the NSE and BSE tentatively set for July 30.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a large, closely-watched listing that tests investor appetite for India's industrial and precision-manufacturing sector, rather than the software or consumer names that usually dominate headlines. Because most of the money raised goes to existing shareholders exiting, not the company itself, it's more a liquidity event for early investors than fresh growth capital for Indo-MIM.
stories.ripple_effects: A well-subscribed IPO here would signal continued strong demand for India's precision-manufacturing story, encouraging more industrial companies to consider going public. A weak listing would be read as a warning sign given India's markets have already been falling for four straight days on oil-driven jitters from the Middle East. Retail investors bidding through UPI-linked applications will find out quickly whether the grey market's early optimism holds up.
stories.why_now: The subscription window opened today, July 23, as previously scheduled by the company and its lead bankers, regardless of the broader market's rocky week.
stories.watch_next: Watch the subscription numbers over the next few days — how many times the issue gets covered — as the clearest early signal of investor appetite, ahead of the July 30 listing.

key_terms.definition: The first time a private company sells shares to the public, so it can be bought and sold on a stock exchange.
Part of an IPO where existing shareholders sell their own shares to the public rather than the company issuing new ones — the money goes to them, not the company.
An unofficial, informal market where an IPO's shares are bought and sold before they officially list, used as an early (unreliable) signal of listing-day demand.

stories.the_lesson: Not every big IPO number means the company is raising that much new money — check how much is a 'fresh issue' versus an 'offer for sale', because that changes what the listing is actually signaling.
stories.market_link: A large, well-subscribed IPO can pull investor cash out of existing listed stocks in the days around its listing, and its debut trading is watched as a barometer of broader market sentiment.

stories.title: India's Parliament is paralyzed for a 4th straight day as Opposition demands the education minister resign over the NEET paper leak
key_points: Rajya Sabha adjourned till 2pm again amid protests
Opposition wants Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation before any NEET debate
Government says it's ready to debate — but won't have him resign first
Comes days after Modi promised fast-track courts for paper leaks
stories.background: NEET is India's national medical entrance exam, taken by over a million students a year to get into medical college — so when a paper leak scandal broke, it hit a nerve nationwide, feeding student protests and a political firestorm. Opposition parties have spent this week's Monsoon Session of Parliament trying to force a debate on the leak and demanding Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan resign, while street protests over the same issue have already led to detentions of Congress leaders and criticism from Human Rights Watch over police conduct.
stories.what_happened: For a fourth consecutive day, both the Lok Sabha (lower house) and Rajya Sabha (upper house) were disrupted and eventually adjourned as opposition MPs demanded Pradhan's resignation as a precondition for debating the NEET paper leak. The Rajya Sabha was adjourned until 2pm on Thursday alone due to the standoff. Parliamentary Affairs Minister Kiren Rijiju said the government is ready to debate the issue without preconditions and accused the Opposition of avoiding the actual debate by insisting on a resignation first.
stories.why_it_matters: A four-day parliamentary logjam means none of the government's other legislative business is moving either — bills, budget items, and other debates are all stuck behind this standoff. It also shows the NEET leak has become a genuine political flashpoint, not just a one-day news story, escalating from street protests into an institutional crisis inside Parliament itself.
stories.ripple_effects: The longer this drags on, the more pressure builds on both sides — the Opposition risks looking obstructionist if it refuses to debate without a resignation, while the government risks looking evasive if it keeps declining calls to hold Pradhan accountable. Students and families anxious about the integrity of medical admissions are left waiting for any real resolution, and the standoff feeds directly into the broader youth protest movement already playing out on Delhi's streets.
stories.why_now: This is the fourth straight day of disruption in the ongoing Monsoon Session (running July 20 to August 13), and follows PM Modi's own public pledge this week to set up fast-track courts for exam paper leaks — a response to the protests that hasn't cooled the standoff in Parliament.
stories.watch_next: Watch whether the Rajya Sabha actually convenes after its 2pm adjournment today, and whether either side blinks before the Monsoon Session's other business runs out of time.

key_terms.definition: India's Parliament has two houses: the Lok Sabha (directly elected lower house) and the Rajya Sabha (upper house, indirectly elected) — both must be adjourned for the day's business to stop entirely.
One of Parliament's three yearly sittings, held during India's monsoon months (roughly July–August).

stories.the_lesson: In parliamentary systems, an opposition can use procedural leverage — refusing to let business proceed — as real power even without the votes to pass anything, because a paralyzed legislature is itself a cost the government wants to avoid.

stories.title: India-US trade deal comes down to the 'last one percent' — with a tariff deadline hours away
key_points: A temporary US tariff arrangement on Indian goods expires Friday, July 24
Negotiators say the first phase of a trade deal is done except for the final 1% of legal text
Rubio and Jaishankar pushed to close the deal despite new US threats on pharma tariffs
The current US tariff on Indian goods sits at 18%, down from a threatened 25%
stories.background: In 2026, the Trump administration has been imposing 'reciprocal tariffs' — import taxes set to match what the US says other countries charge American goods — as leverage in trade talks. India and the US have spent months negotiating an interim Bilateral Trade Agreement, with Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal and US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer haggling over market access, agriculture and non-tariff barriers, under a shared 'Mission 500' goal of doubling two-way trade to $500 billion by 2030.
stories.what_happened: With a temporary US tariff arrangement on Indian goods set to expire Friday, July 24, negotiators describe the first phase of the deal's legal text as parked at its 'last one percent' — essentially finished but not yet signed. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and India's External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar reinforced the push to close the interim agreement, even as Washington separately threatens new tariffs on pharmaceutical imports. The reciprocal tariff on Indian goods currently stands at 18%, already reduced from an earlier 25% threat.
stories.why_it_matters: This is the deal that determines whether Indian exporters — pharmaceuticals, textiles, IT-linked goods — face a favorable or punitive US tariff regime starting this weekend. The pharma tariff threat directly overlaps with today's news of Cipla's US generic-drug sales sliding on tariff fears, showing how this single negotiation ripples through specific Indian companies' earnings.
stories.ripple_effects: A signed deal would remove a major overhang for Indian pharma and export-linked stocks already under pressure this week from oil prices and FII selling; a missed deadline extends uncertainty and could add another drag on Sensex and Nifty, which have now fallen for four straight sessions.
stories.why_now: The temporary US tariff arrangement expires tomorrow, July 24, forcing both sides to either finalize the deal or let steeper tariffs kick back in.
stories.watch_next: Whether the deal is signed before the July 24 deadline, or whether talks spill past it into an extended tariff period.

key_terms.definition: An import tax the US sets to match what it says a trading partner charges on American goods — used here as negotiating leverage.
A trade deal negotiated directly between two countries, as opposed to a multi-country trade bloc agreement.

stories.the_lesson: Trade deals that are '99% done' can still stall for months on the final technical details — a looming deadline creates pressure to finish, but doesn't guarantee it happens on time.
stories.market_link: A missed deadline would extend tariff uncertainty for Indian pharma and export stocks, adding to the pressure already visible in this week's four-session Sensex/Nifty slide.

stories.title: Commonwealth Games 2026 open in Glasgow — Mirabai Chanu carries India's flag, Lovlina Borgohain the baton
key_points: The Commonwealth Games officially open today at Glasgow's Hydro arena
Weightlifter Mirabai Chanu is India's flag bearer, boxer Lovlina Borgohain the baton bearer
Chanu is a two-time Commonwealth Games gold medallist; Borgohain is chasing her first CWG medal
India's contingent had already begun competing hours earlier in lawn bowls
stories.background: The Commonwealth Games is a multi-sport event held every four years among athletes from Commonwealth nations (mostly former British territories, including India, the UK, Australia and dozens of others). Glasgow last hosted in 2014 and steps in again for 2026. Carrying the flag and baton at the opening ceremony is one of the highest honors a nation gives its athletes — Chanu is a Tokyo Olympics silver medallist and two-time CWG champion (2018, 2022), while Borgohain is a Tokyo bronze medallist still seeking her first Commonwealth Games medal.
stories.what_happened: India's contingent, led by flag bearer Mirabai Chanu and baton bearer Lovlina Borgohain, marched in the opening ceremony at Glasgow's Hydro arena on Thursday, formally starting the 2026 Commonwealth Games. Indian athletes were already in action earlier the same day, with the lawn bowls pair opening India's campaign in Glasgow.
stories.why_it_matters: The opening ceremony marks the start of roughly 11 days of competition where India will field one of its largest overseas Games contingents, across sports from weightlifting and boxing to athletics and bowls. It's also a chance to reset the narrative after this week's news that India's weightlifting quota was cut following doping positives — the Games now shift focus to the athletes competing clean.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong Indian medal haul boosts sponsorship interest, broadcast viewership and grassroots funding for these sports back home; a weak one renews scrutiny on preparation and anti-doping systems, especially in weightlifting after this week's positives.
stories.why_now: The quadrennial Games begin today, and the flag/baton bearer announcement always lands right at the opening ceremony as a symbolic curtain-raiser for the host nation's competing delegation.
stories.watch_next: India's first medal events across bowls, weightlifting and boxing over the opening days of competition.

key_terms.definition: A multi-sport event held every four years for athletes from Commonwealth nations — a global sporting event roughly comparable in scale to a mid-sized Olympics.

stories.the_lesson: Multi-nation sporting events carry real diplomatic and reputational weight beyond the scoreboard — who a country picks to carry its flag is itself a statement about which achievements and values it wants the world to see first.

stories.title: The Supreme Court refuses to block 6 defecting MPs from merging into Eknath Shinde's Shiv Sena, dealing Uddhav Thackeray a fresh setback
key_points: The Lok Sabha Speaker had approved 6 Shiv Sena (UBT) MPs merging with the rival Shinde faction
The Supreme Court refused to stay that decision while the case is heard
The merger shrinks Uddhav Thackeray's Lok Sabha bloc to 3 MPs, growing Shinde's to 13
The court will properly hear the challenge in two weeks, but the merger stands for now
stories.background: The original Shiv Sena party split in 2022 when Eknath Shinde led a rebellion of MPs and MLAs away from then-chief-minister Uddhav Thackeray, eventually taking Shinde's faction into power in Maharashtra with the backing of the BJP. Both factions have since fought in courts and before the Election Commission over which one gets to keep the Shiv Sena name and symbol. India's anti-defection law is meant to stop elected members from switching parties after winning on one party's ticket, but a group of members can be exempted if it counts as a formal "merger" — a process that requires certification, in this case by Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla.
stories.what_happened: On Wednesday, a Supreme Court bench of Justices P.S. Narasimha and Alok Aradhe declined to grant an interim stay on Speaker Om Birla's July 18 decision recognizing the merger of six Shiv Sena (UBT) MPs into the Shinde-led Shiv Sena. The court did issue formal notice to the Speaker's office, seeking a response, and scheduled a full hearing in two weeks. Shiv Sena (UBT) had argued the merger order was improperly signed by a Joint Secretary rather than the Speaker himself, and that no disqualification petitions were even pending. With the Speaker's decision left standing for now, Shinde's Shiv Sena grows from 7 to 13 Lok Sabha MPs, while Thackeray's UBT faction falls to just 3.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a real-time test of how much protection India's anti-defection law actually offers a party whose members want to jump ship — and how much power a Speaker's certification carries, even before a court has fully examined whether it was done correctly. For Thackeray, it's a further practical and symbolic loss in the multi-year fight over the Shiv Sena's legacy.
stories.ripple_effects: The Shinde faction's expanded Lok Sabha numbers strengthen the ruling coalition's hand nationally, however marginally, while the case sets a precedent other defecting legislators elsewhere in India may watch closely. Uddhav Thackeray's political relevance, already reduced since the 2022 split, takes another hit.
stories.why_now: The Speaker's certification and Shiv Sena (UBT)'s emergency Supreme Court challenge both happened within the past few days, as the party tried to get the merger blocked before it could take practical effect.
stories.watch_next: The full Supreme Court hearing in roughly two weeks, where the validity of the Speaker's certification process will be argued in depth.

key_terms.definition: An Indian law meant to stop elected legislators from switching parties after being elected, though formal party mergers involving enough members can be exempted from it.
The presiding officer of India's lower house of Parliament, who also rules on disputes like whether a group of MPs counts as a legitimate party merger.

stories.the_lesson: In politics, procedural authority — who gets to certify a fact, and when — often matters as much as the underlying merits of a dispute. Courts are frequently reluctant to freeze a decision immediately, which lets it take real effect on the ground long before a final legal verdict arrives.

stories.title: Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi are detained marching on Modi's residence, as Human Rights Watch confirms 'excessive force' against India's student protesters
key_points: Congress leaders marched on PM Modi's residence; Rahul Gandhi was dragged away and detained
The march protested Monday's police crackdown on the youth-led 'Cockroach' protests
Human Rights Watch has now independently confirmed security forces used excessive force
HRW is calling for an investigation and an end to mobile internet shutdowns during protests
stories.background: India's 'Cockroach' movement began in June 2026 after the Chief Justice of India compared unemployed, protesting youth to "cockroaches" and "parasites." It grew into a nationwide challenge to the government over repeated exam-paper leaks (including the medical-entrance NEET exam) and demands for Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation. A march toward Parliament on July 20 was met with tear gas and batons; Delhi Police say roughly 180 people were hurt. Days earlier, opposition MPs had worn black clothing into Parliament and Congress leader Rahul Gandhi demanded apologies and resignations from PM Modi and Home Minister Amit Shah over the crackdown — a demand the government had not acted on.
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday evening, Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge, Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra led a march from Kharge's residence toward Prime Minister Modi's residence on Lok Kalyan Marg, demanding accountability for the police crackdown. When police blocked the march, Rahul Gandhi sat down on the road and refused to move; he was forcibly dragged away and detained, along with Priyanka Gandhi and Samajwadi Party chief Akhilesh Yadav. Two days later, on July 23, Human Rights Watch published its own investigation — based on interviews with nine witnesses and verification of ten videos — concluding Indian security forces used unnecessary and excessive force against what were largely peaceful student protesters on July 20, and calling on the Modi government to investigate the crackdown and stop suspending mobile internet during protests.
stories.why_it_matters: This has escalated from a domestic parliamentary protest into a direct, physical confrontation between India's top opposition leaders and the police, now backed by an international human-rights investigation. That combination raises the political cost for the government of ignoring the protesters' demands, and puts India's handling of dissent under outside scrutiny.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect continued disruption to Parliament's ongoing session as the opposition presses the issue, more pressure on Education Minister Pradhan and Home Minister Shah, and closer international media attention on India's protest-policing record. It also keeps activist Sonam Wangchuk's hunger strike (over the same crackdown) in the spotlight as a linked flashpoint.
stories.why_now: Human Rights Watch's report, published today, is the first major independent international assessment of the July 20 crackdown, arriving just as the political standoff between Congress and the government was already at its most intense.
stories.watch_next: Whether the government responds to HRW's call for an investigation, and whether Parliament's monsoon session can resume normal business or remains gridlocked over the issue.

key_terms.definition: A youth-led Indian protest movement demanding accountability for exam-paper leaks, named after the Chief Justice's dismissive comparison of protesting youth to cockroaches.
India's National Eligibility cum Entrance Test — the single, high-stakes exam that decides admission to medical colleges nationwide, whose repeated leaks triggered the protests.

stories.the_lesson: When a government doesn't address a protest movement's core grievance, the protest doesn't fade — it escalates and finds new institutional pressure points (Parliament, the streets, international rights groups) until someone with real leverage forces a response.

thread.previously: Opposition MPs wore black into Parliament to protest the July 20 crackdown, with Rahul Gandhi demanding an apology from PM Modi and the resignations of Amit Shah and Dharmendra Pradhan.
thread.changed: Gandhi escalated from a parliamentary protest to physically marching on Modi's residence, where he was detained; two days later, Human Rights Watch independently confirmed security forces used excessive force against the student protesters.

stories.title: India's weightlifting quota at the Commonwealth Games is slashed after five Indian lifters test positive for doping, sending Dilbag Singh home
key_points: Five Indian weightlifters tested positive for doping between June 2025 and July 2026
The violations cut India's weightlifting quota at CWG 2026 from 16 places to 11
Dilbag Singh (men's 94kg) and judoka Arun Kumar are among those withdrawn
Dilbag himself was not among those who failed a test — he's a quota casualty
stories.background: The Commonwealth Games, a multi-sport event for athletes from Commonwealth nations, allocates each country a fixed number of competitor slots per sport based on qualification performances. Anti-doping violations by a country's athletes in the run-up to a Games can trigger quota reductions as a penalty, even for athletes who personally tested clean — a rule meant to hold national federations collectively accountable for doping culture.
stories.what_happened: Five Indian weightlifters tested positive for doping violations in the period from June 1, 2025 to July 22, 2026, triggering a rule that cut India's weightlifting quota for the 2026 Commonwealth Games in Glasgow from 16 places down to 11. As a result, weightlifter Dilbag Singh, who competes in the men's 94kg category, was withdrawn from India's squad, along with judoka Arun Kumar. Dilbag was not personally implicated in any doping case — reports note he had also been struggling with a back injury and had been unable to train fully during India's preparatory camp in Birmingham, making the call a difficult but practical one.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a real cost of India's ongoing doping problem in weightlifting, a sport where the country has historically won a large share of its Commonwealth Games medals. Clean athletes are now losing chances to compete because of violations they had no part in, which puts pressure on the Indian Weightlifting Federation to fix its testing and enforcement well before future Games.
stories.ripple_effects: Fewer weightlifting medal chances for India at these Games could dent its overall medal count, since weightlifting has been one of India's most productive Commonwealth Games sports. It also adds to scrutiny of Indian sports federations from global anti-doping bodies, which can affect funding, athlete eligibility, and India's standing when bidding to host future events.
stories.why_now: The quota cut lands right as the Commonwealth Games open in Glasgow this week, forcing India to finalize and send home affected athletes just as the competition begins.
stories.watch_next: Watch whether the Indian Weightlifting Federation announces disciplinary or testing reforms in response, and how the remaining 11 Indian lifters perform under the reduced quota.

key_terms.definition: A penalty where a country's number of allowed competitors in a sport is cut because of doping violations by its athletes, regardless of whether every affected athlete personally doped.

stories.the_lesson: Anti-doping penalties are often collective, not just individual — a federation's failure to prevent doping can cost clean athletes their spot, which is designed to push national sports bodies to police themselves rather than let violations slide.

stories.title: Jaishankar tells China's Wang Yi that fixing trade imbalance and market access is key to normalizing ties, as the two meet on the sidelines in Manila
key_points: Jaishankar and Wang Yi met on the sidelines of a regional ASEAN gathering in Manila
Jaishankar raised fair market access and India's trade imbalance with China
He repeated that border peace remains a 'prerequisite' for normal ties
Comes as India and China have been slowly easing tensions since their 2020 border clash
stories.background: India and China fought a deadly military clash along their disputed Himalayan border in Galwan Valley in 2020, which froze high-level ties and trade cooperation for years. Since late 2024 the two sides have been cautiously de-escalating — restoring some direct flights and border trade — but deep mistrust remains, and India runs a large and growing trade deficit with China, importing far more (mostly electronics and machinery components) than it exports.
stories.what_happened: India's External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar met his Chinese counterpart Wang Yi on the sidelines of a regional foreign ministers' gathering in Manila on Wednesday. Jaishankar said the two countries needed to address issues like fair market access and the trade balance as part of repairing relations, and reiterated that peace and tranquility along the disputed border remains a prerequisite for any return to normal ties.
stories.why_it_matters: This signals India wants economic issues — not just the military border dispute — on the table with China, reflecting how dependent Indian industry has become on Chinese components even amid political hostility. It's also a sign India is willing to keep talking to China even while deepening ties with the US, showing New Delhi's preference for keeping multiple major-power relationships open rather than picking a side.
stories.ripple_effects: Indian manufacturers reliant on Chinese components (especially electronics and solar) would benefit from smoother trade ties, but domestic industry groups pushing 'Make in India' self-reliance may push back on any opening. Progress here could unlock stalled Chinese investment proposals in India, while a stall could keep tensions simmering ahead of any Modi-Xi engagement.
stories.why_now: The meeting happened on the sidelines of a broader flurry of Asia-Pacific diplomacy in Manila this week, where China's Wang Yi is also holding separate talks with the US and Russia — giving India a chance to press its own agenda while major powers are already gathered.
stories.watch_next: Watch for any follow-up announcement on easing visa, investment, or direct-flight restrictions between India and China in the coming weeks.

key_terms.definition: When a country buys much more from a trading partner than it sells to them — India imports far more from China than it exports back.

stories.the_lesson: Even bitter geopolitical rivals keep economic channels open when the trade dependency runs deep enough — border hostility and business ties can coexist rather than one automatically shutting down the other.
stories.market_link: Indian electronics-assembly and solar-manufacturing stocks that rely on Chinese components would be sensitive to any easing of trade friction.

stories.title: Zomato and Blinkit's parent Eternal posts profit up 268%, as its 10-minute grocery arm turns profitable for the first time
key_points: Consolidated net profit up 268% YoY to ₹92 crore
Revenue up 182% YoY to ₹20,211 crore
Blinkit (quick commerce) swung from an EBITDA loss to a ₹102 crore profit
Blinkit is now Eternal's single biggest revenue source, ahead of food delivery
stories.background: Eternal is the parent company of Zomato (food delivery) and Blinkit (10-minute grocery delivery) — renamed from Zomato Limited last year. Blinkit and India's other 'quick commerce' apps have spent heavily building dark stores (small local warehouses) to promise 10-minute deliveries, burning cash for years to grab market share ahead of rivals like Swiggy's Instamart and Zepto.
stories.what_happened: Eternal reported Q1 FY27 (April-June 2026) consolidated net profit of ₹92 crore, up 268% from ₹25 crore a year earlier, on revenue that grew 182% to ₹20,211 crore. Blinkit alone brought in ₹15,664 crore of that revenue — more than six times what it made a year ago — and swung from a ₹162 crore loss to a ₹102 crore profit at the adjusted-EBITDA level, adding 899 new dark stores over the year. Core Zomato food delivery grew a steadier 37% to ₹3,100 crore.
stories.why_it_matters: For years the market questioned whether India's quick-commerce race was just a cash-burning subsidy war with no path to profit. This quarter is the clearest signal yet that Blinkit — the largest player — can make money at scale, which changes the investment case for the entire sector, including rivals Swiggy and Zepto.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect renewed investor appetite for quick-commerce and food-delivery stocks, pressure on Swiggy to show a similar profitability turn in its own results, and less room for smaller quick-commerce players who can't match Blinkit's scale to keep raising cash on 'growth-over-profit' pitches.
stories.why_now: This is Eternal's first quarterly report since the sharp swing to profit became fully visible, based on numbers filed with exchanges this week — the market has been watching Blinkit's unit economics closely since the segment first broke even at a smaller scale last quarter.
stories.watch_next: Whether Blinkit's profitability holds as it keeps opening new dark stores, and how Swiggy's Instamart results (due out separately) compare.

key_terms.definition: Grocery and essentials delivery promised within 10-30 minutes, using a dense network of small local warehouses ('dark stores') instead of large distribution centres — it costs more to run than traditional e-commerce but wins on speed.

A company's profit from operations before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation, adjusted for one-off items — often used for young, fast-growing companies to show whether the core business is turning a profit even if the full accounting profit isn't yet.

stories.the_lesson: A business that loses money for years while growing fast isn't necessarily unprofitable by nature — sometimes it's genuinely still investing in scale, and the real test is whether profit shows up once growth slows down enough for the model to prove itself.
stories.market_link: Eternal (formerly Zomato) and quick-commerce-linked peers (Swiggy, Zepto's investors) directly react to this — it's a read-through for the whole quick-commerce sector's path to profitability.

stories.title: Assam floods kill 10 more in a day, pushing the week's death toll to 36 as 650,000 people are hit across 11 districts
key_points: 10 killed in Assam in the last 24 hours alone
State's flood death toll for the week reaches 36
650,000+ people affected across 11 districts
Trigger: extreme rain fell in neighbouring Nagaland on July 18-20
stories.background: Assam sits on the Brahmaputra river's floodplain and floods almost every monsoon, but authorities are calling this week's flooding 'unprecedented.' It follows days of very heavy rain in the hills of neighbouring Nagaland, which drained down into Assam's rivers and pushed them over their banks. A separate weather system has also brought deadly flooding this week to parts of Pakistan and Afghanistan.
stories.what_happened: Assam recorded at least 10 more flood deaths in the 24 hours to Thursday, taking the state's death toll for the week to 36. More than 650,000 people have been affected by flooding across 11 districts, with authorities calling the scale of damage to farmland, homes and infrastructure 'unprecedented.' Separately, a mother and son were swept away and killed by fast-moving floodwater in Bandipora district of Jammu & Kashmir.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a live humanitarian emergency, not a forecast — real deaths and hundreds of thousands of people already displaced or cut off, on top of the IMD's separate heavy-rain warning for 12 more states over the next 24 hours. Assam's floods regularly damage the state's tea and rice harvests, a meaningful part of its economy.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect disruption to Assam's tea gardens and paddy fields during a critical growing window, pressure on the state government to speed up relief and embankment repairs, and continued strain on India's disaster-response machinery as the IMD's broader red alert plays out at the same time.
stories.why_now: Unusually heavy rain fell over Nagaland between July 18-20 and has been draining downstream into Assam's rivers since, compounding with this week's separate red-alert weather system to produce the worst 24-hour toll of the season so far.
stories.watch_next: Whether river levels in the Brahmaputra basin peak and recede over the next few days, or whether the IMD's fresh 12-state rain warning adds a new surge upstream.

key_terms.definition: The low-lying land alongside the Brahmaputra river in Assam that floods almost every monsoon because the river carries an enormous volume of water down from the Himalayas and Nagaland hills.

stories.the_lesson: Flood disasters are rarely caused only by rain falling where the damage happens — water that falls upstream, in a different state, can drown people days later, which is why regional, not just local, weather matters for disaster planning.

stories.title: Meesho halves its losses and grows revenue 48% in its first results as a listed company
key_points: Net loss narrows 54% year-on-year to ₹132.8 crore, from ₹289.3 crore
Revenue jumps 48% to ₹3,713 crore
Transacting users up 29% to 274 million; orders up 29% to 725 million
Reported the same day as Infosys, IndiGo, Cipla and Dr Reddy's Q1 results
stories.background: Meesho is India's social-commerce and value e-commerce platform, built around reaching small-town and rural 'Bharat' shoppers rather than big-city customers, and recently listed on Indian stock exchanges. Like other new-age internet listings such as Paytm and Zomato's parent Eternal, investors are watching closely for a credible path to profitability.
stories.what_happened: Meesho's revenue from operations rose to ₹3,713 crore from ₹2,504 crore a year earlier, while its net loss shrank to ₹132.8 crore from ₹289.3 crore. Its EBITDA loss (earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization — a measure of core operating losses before accounting adjustments) also narrowed, to ₹224.7 crore from ₹264.4 crore.
stories.why_it_matters: It's an early data point on whether India's loss-making new-age internet companies can grow into profitability rather than just grow — the same question hanging over Paytm, Eternal and Swiggy.
stories.ripple_effects: A narrowing-loss, rising-revenue result tends to move sentiment across the whole basket of new-age listed Indian tech companies, since investors often treat them as one trade on 'Is Indian consumer-tech profitable yet?'
stories.why_now: It's quarterly (Q1 FY27) earnings season, when most major Indian companies report results within the same two-to-three week window.
stories.watch_next: Whether Meesho's loss keeps narrowing at this pace toward an eventual profit, and how its stock reacts relative to peers reporting the same week.

key_terms.definition: An Indian counting unit equal to 10 million — so ₹100 crore is ₹1 billion.
A company's earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization — a way to see how a core business is performing before financing and accounting choices are factored in.

stories.the_lesson: For loss-making new-age internet companies, the market often rewards the trajectory — a shrinking loss alongside rising revenue — as much as the absolute number, because it signals a believable route to eventual profit.
stories.market_link: Meesho's results, and how its NSE-listed stock reacts, tend to move sentiment toward peers like Eternal (Zomato) and Paytm as investors gauge whether India's new-age consumer-tech listings are nearing profitability.

stories.title: PM Modi announces fast-track courts for exam paper leaks, as the injured protester who galvanized the 'Cockroach' movement is taken off ventilator
key_points: Modi announces fast-track courts to punish paper-leak culprits: 'no one will be spared'
Comes three days after the Cockroach Janta Party's Parliament march turned violent
The 21-year-old student critically hurt in the police crackdown is now off ventilator, conscious
CNBC reports the youth movement is now seen as a direct test of Modi's political standing
stories.background: The Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) began as a social-media joke mocking a judge's remark comparing unemployed youth to 'cockroaches,' then grew into a genuine youth movement demanding accountability for the NEET medical-entrance exam paper-leak scandal and the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan. A march on Parliament last week ended in clashes, with over 118 police and around 60 protesters injured and a 21-year-old woman critically hurt after colliding with a barricade during the crackdown.
stories.what_happened: Modi announced the government will set up fast-track courts specifically to deliver swift, stringent punishment in paper-leak cases, saying 'nothing is more important than the welfare and future of the youth' and 'no one who tampers with the future of our youth will be spared.' Separately, Delhi's RML Hospital confirmed the critically injured student protester has been extubated, is conscious and responding normally, with a normal brain and spine MRI.
stories.why_it_matters: It's the government's first concrete concession to a protest movement that has run for over three weeks and forced the state to change its posture from confrontation toward at least partial accommodation — without meeting the protesters' central demand that the education minister resign.
stories.ripple_effects: How this plays out will shape whether CJP's momentum continues or fades, affects Education Minister Pradhan's political position, and sets a precedent for how the government handles future exam-integrity scandals — a recurring flashpoint in Indian politics given how many careers ride on competitive exams.
stories.why_now: Three weeks of sustained protests, a march that turned violent, and national attention on a critically injured student created enough political pressure that the government needed a visible response before the story became purely about police conduct.
stories.watch_next: Whether the Cockroach Janta Party calls off further marches now that fast-track courts have been announced, and whether Dharmendra Pradhan's position as education minister changes.

key_terms.definition: A special court set up to hear and decide a specific category of cases much faster than India's normally backlogged court system.

stories.the_lesson: Governments under street pressure often move first on process — a new court, a new rule — because it's faster and cheaper than the personnel change protesters actually want, even though it may not by itself end the anger driving the protest.

thread.previously: We explained how the Cockroach Janta Party movement began as a social-media joke mocking a judge's remark about unemployed youth, and grew into a mass youth movement demanding accountability for the NEET paper-leak scandal and the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan.
thread.changed: PM Modi has now directly responded by announcing fast-track courts for paper-leak cases, while the 21-year-old protester critically injured in last week's police crackdown at Jantar Mantar has been taken off ventilator support and is recovering — and CNBC reports the movement is now seen as a genuine test of Modi's political leadership.

prev_ref.title: India's 'Cockroach' movement, explained: how a joke about students became a nationwide challenge to Modi

stories.title: IndiGo swings to a ₹238 crore loss as fuel costs jump 86% — even as India's biggest airline keeps growing revenue
key_points: Revenue grew 20% year-on-year to ₹24,584 crore
But total costs climbed 34%, driven by an 86% jump in fuel expenses
Net loss of ₹238 crore, versus a ₹2,176 crore profit a year ago
IndiGo still holds roughly 65% of India's domestic flight market
stories.background: IndiGo (run by InterGlobe Aviation) is India's largest airline by far, carrying roughly two-thirds of all domestic air passengers. Airlines are extremely sensitive to fuel prices because jet fuel is typically their single biggest cost — and India's fuel costs are also shaped by global crude oil prices, which have been rising due to ongoing Middle East tensions, including the Iran-related conflict escalating in the region.
stories.what_happened: IndiGo reported a consolidated net loss of ₹238 crore for the quarter ended June 2026, compared with a ₹2,176 crore profit in the same quarter last year. Revenue actually grew 20% to ₹24,584 crore on strong travel demand, but total expenditure rose even faster — up 34% to ₹25,853 crore — mainly because fuel costs jumped 86% year-on-year. The airline still ended the quarter with ₹52,885 crore in cash, giving it room to absorb the hit.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a direct, concrete example of how the oil-price spike rattling global markets this week (driven by Iran-related tensions and Red Sea tanker attacks) is already hitting real Indian companies and, by extension, consumers — airlines facing higher costs typically pass some of that through to ticket prices over time.
stories.ripple_effects: Watch other Indian airlines (SpiceJet, Air India) for similar cost pressure in their own results. If fuel costs stay elevated, expect airfares to rise heading into the festive travel season later this year, and airline stocks broadly to stay under pressure alongside the rest of the market, which is already down for a fourth straight session on oil-driven jitters.
stories.why_now: IndiGo's Q1 FY27 (April-June 2026) results were released today alongside dozens of other Indian companies reporting quarterly earnings this week, and land right as crude oil prices have broken out on Middle East supply fears.
stories.watch_next: Whether IndiGo raises fares or fuel surcharges in response, and how peer airlines' upcoming results compare.

key_terms.definition: The total loss for a company and all the subsidiaries it controls, combined into one number — a fuller picture than the parent company alone.

stories.the_lesson: A business can grow its revenue strongly and still lose money if its costs — especially ones tied to volatile inputs like fuel — grow even faster. Revenue growth alone doesn't tell you whether a company is becoming more or less profitable.
stories.market_link: InterGlobe Aviation (INDIGO) shares are in focus; the story ties directly into the crude-oil rally pressuring the broader Nifty and Sensex this week.

stories.title: Kuldeep Yadav signs with Yorkshire, becoming only the fifth Indian to play for the county
key_points: Kuldeep joins Yorkshire as an overseas player for the One-Day Cup and County Championship
Plays five One-Day Cup matches from today before leaving for India's Sri Lanka Test tour
Returns in September for three more County Championship matches
Joins a short list of Indians for Yorkshire: Tendulkar, Yuvraj, Pujara, Mayank Agarwal
stories.background: India's left-arm wrist-spinner Kuldeep Yadav has recently found himself in and out of India's white-ball plans, missing out on some Team India selections. English county cricket has long been a place where international players — including a handful of high-profile Indians — sign short overseas stints to stay match-fit and sharpen their game between international assignments, playing in England's domestic first-class (County Championship) and one-day (One-Day Cup) competitions.
stories.what_happened: Kuldeep Yadav has signed an overseas deal with Yorkshire, one of English cricket's most storied county clubs. He'll play five One-Day Cup matches starting July 24, before leaving to join India's squad for their two-Test tour of Sri Lanka in August. He'll then return to Yorkshire in September to play the club's final three County Championship (first-class) matches of the season. He becomes only the fifth Indian to represent Yorkshire, following Sachin Tendulkar, Yuvraj Singh, Cheteshwar Pujara, and Mayank Agarwal.
stories.why_it_matters: For Kuldeep, a county stint is a chance to get consistent match time and bowl in different conditions while he works his way back into India's selection picture, especially with red-ball cricket (the County Championship leg) on offer — a format where he gets fewer international opportunities. Being only the fifth Indian to play for Yorkshire also places him in genuinely elite company historically.
stories.ripple_effects: A good showing for Yorkshire could strengthen his case for India's Test or white-ball squads in the near future, particularly since red-ball form is harder to demonstrate when a player isn't in India's regular XI. It's also a reminder of how county cricket functions as an unofficial fitness-and-form pipeline that feeds back into international selection.
stories.why_now: The timing lines up with a gap in Kuldeep's international calendar — after missing recent India selections and before India's tour of Sri Lanka in August — making this county window available to him right now.
stories.watch_next: How Kuldeep performs across his five One-Day Cup matches, and whether it factors into India's squad selection for future series.

key_terms.definition: England's premier first-class (multi-day) domestic cricket competition, the traditional proving ground for red-ball form in English cricket.
A non-English player signed by a county club for a limited period, typically to boost the squad's quality or fill a specific role like a specialist spinner.

stories.the_lesson: For international players temporarily outside their national team's plans, domestic leagues in other countries often serve as a proving ground — a place to rack up form and match fitness that selectors can point to when a recall comes up.

stories.title: Sri Lanka captain Kusal Mendis ruled out of LPL with a hamstring injury, clouding India Test series
key_points: Mendis hurt his hamstring running between the wickets against Kandy Royals on July 19
Ruled out of the rest of Lanka Premier League 2026; Kamindu Mendis takes over as Colombo Kaps captain
Raises doubts over his fitness for Sri Lanka's two-Test series against India starting August 15
He's now back at the National High Performance Centre beginning rehab
stories.background: Kusal Mendis is Sri Lanka's first-choice wicketkeeper-batter and was captaining Colombo Kaps in this year's Lanka Premier League (LPL), the country's domestic T20 franchise tournament. He's also a key part of Sri Lanka's squad for a high-profile two-Test home series against India starting August 15 — a series that matters a lot for Sri Lankan cricket given how rarely they get to host India in Tests.
stories.what_happened: Mendis suffered a right hamstring injury while attempting a run during Colombo Kaps' LPL match against Kandy Royals on July 19. He tried to continue but was forced to retire hurt after a medical assessment, two overs into the run chase. He has now been ruled out of the remainder of the 2026 LPL, with Kamindu Mendis stepping in as Kaps captain, while Kusal begins a rehabilitation program at Sri Lanka's National High Performance Centre.
stories.why_it_matters: Beyond the immediate loss to his franchise team, this puts a genuine question mark over whether Sri Lanka's captain and best batter will be fit for the India Test series just three weeks away — a series many Sri Lankan fans have been anticipating.
stories.ripple_effects: If Mendis isn't fit in time, Sri Lanka would have to reshuffle both its captaincy and batting order for a marquee series against a strong Indian side, which could affect team balance and confidence going in. It also puts pressure on Sri Lanka Cricket's medical staff to get a precise recovery timeline before squad selection.
stories.why_now: The injury happened mid-tournament, and news of the fitness doubt over the upcoming Test series broke as Sri Lanka Cricket and fans started asking whether a hamstring strain would heal in time for August 15.
stories.watch_next: An official fitness update from Sri Lanka Cricket on whether Mendis will be available for the first Test against India.

key_terms.definition: When an injured batter has to leave the field mid-innings without being dismissed — they can return to bat later in the same innings if fit enough.

stories.the_lesson: In a sport with a packed calendar of overlapping franchise and international cricket, a single injury in a domestic T20 game can ripple straight into a country's plans for a marquee international series weeks later — the schedules are all connected even when the tournaments aren't.

stories.title: Pakistan's U19 women level their series 2-2 against South Africa — decider set for today in Karachi
key_points: Pakistan's U19 women beat South Africa's U19 women by 6 wickets in the 4th match
That win levelled the five-match youth T20I series 2-2
Zoofishan Ayyaz (44 not out) and captain Fizza Fiaz (28 not out) shared an unbeaten 70-run stand
The series decider (5th T20I) is scheduled for today, July 24, in Karachi
stories.background: This is an unofficial five-match Youth T20I series between Pakistan's and South Africa's Under-19 women's teams, played in Karachi — part of the pipeline that develops future senior international players on both sides.
stories.what_happened: Chasing South Africa's 109 for 8, Pakistan's U19 women were nearly derailed by leg-spinner Respect Mabasa's three wickets, but an unbeaten 70-run partnership between Zoofishan Ayyaz (44 not out) and captain Fizza Fiaz (28 not out) got them home by six wickets with four balls to spare, levelling the series at 2-2 heading into Friday's decider.
stories.why_it_matters: Youth series like this are where the next generation of Pakistan and South Africa women's cricketers get tested against quality opposition — several players from past U19 series have gone on to represent their senior national sides.
stories.ripple_effects: A series win for either side heading into the rest of the cricketing calendar is a confidence and selection marker for their senior set-ups' future plans.
stories.why_now: This was the fourth of five scheduled matches in the series, played Thursday, setting up today's decider.
stories.watch_next: Watch the 5th T20I decider today (July 24) in Karachi, broadcast on the Pakistan Cricket Board's YouTube channel.
stories.the_lesson: Youth international series are a leading indicator worth watching — the players standing out here are often the senior internationals fans will recognize in a few years.

stories.title: Manchester Super Giants edge London Spirit by 7 runs at Lord's as Livingstone stars in a losing cause
key_points: Manchester Super Giants beat London Spirit by 7 runs in The Hundred at Lord's
Liam Livingstone starred for the losing side: 69 runs plus 2 wickets, named Match Hero
Jos Buttler and Aiden Markram's Super Giants held on despite Livingstone's fightback
A separate match from the earlier Women's Hundred game at Lord's, which Super Giants also won
stories.background: The Hundred is England's short-form franchise cricket competition — each side bats 100 balls (not the usual 120 in T20) across eight-team men's and women's leagues that run in parallel every English summer. Manchester Super Giants and London Spirit had already met earlier the same day in the women's competition, also at Lord's — this was the separate men's fixture.
stories.what_happened: Manchester Super Giants, captained by Aiden Markram and featuring Jos Buttler, beat London Spirit by 7 runs in the third men's match of The Hundred 2026 at Lord's on Thursday. London Spirit's Liam Livingstone was the standout individual performer — hitting 69 runs and taking 2 wickets for 14 runs — but couldn't get enough support from teammates to complete the chase, and was named Match Hero despite the loss.
stories.why_it_matters: It's an early marker in the men's Hundred table for two of the competition's stronger squads, and continues Manchester Super Giants' strong start across both the men's and women's competitions this week.
stories.ripple_effects: Points in The Hundred's short round-robin format matter disproportionately, since there are few games before playoffs — an early win like this helps Super Giants' net-run-rate and standings position.
stories.why_now: This was Thursday's scheduled fixture in The Hundred's 2026 men's competition calendar.
stories.watch_next: Watch whether Livingstone's Spirit can turn strong individual performances into wins as the group stage continues.

key_terms.definition: An England-based franchise cricket tournament where each team faces 100 balls per innings instead of the usual 120 in T20 cricket.

stories.the_lesson: In short-format cricket, one brilliant individual innings often isn't enough without support — team depth usually beats a single standout performance.

stories.title: West Indies name their Test squad for Pakistan, with a first-ever Test venue and a fresh face in the spin attack
key_points: Roston Chase captains a 15-man squad; Jomel Warrican is vice-captain
Left-arm spinner Joshua Bishop gets his maiden Test call-up
Kirk McKenzie returns, replacing the injured John Campbell
The opening Test at Trinidad's Brian Lara Academy will be the ground's first-ever men's Test
stories.background: West Indies cricket has been rebuilding in recent years, and this squad largely keeps the core group that just won a Test series against Sri Lanka. Roston Chase, primarily known as an all-rounder, has been entrusted with the captaincy for this series.
stories.what_happened: Cricket West Indies (CWI) announced its 15-player squad for a two-match home Test series against Pakistan, running from July 25 to August 6. Joshua Bishop, a left-arm orthodox spinner who has taken 91 wickets in regional cricket for Barbados Pride and the West Indies Academy over the last three seasons, earns his first Test call-up. Kirk McKenzie returns to the squad after last playing a Test in 2024, replacing opener John Campbell, who is out with a left hamstring strain suffered during the recent ODI series against New Zealand. The first Test will be played at the Brian Lara Cricket Academy in Trinidad — the venue's first-ever men's Test match.
stories.why_it_matters: Blooding a specialist spinner and giving a new venue its first Test both matter for West Indies' longer-term rebuilding — spin depth and expanding the pool of Test venues have both been in short supply for the board in recent years.
stories.ripple_effects: A strong debut from Bishop would boost West Indies' spin options for future series in Asia, where the team has historically struggled; a successful first Test at the Brian Lara Academy could put the ground in line for more regular international fixtures.
stories.why_now: The squad announcement comes two days before the series opener on July 25, standard timing for a home series, right after the same core group won its just-completed series against Sri Lanka 1-0.
stories.watch_next: How Joshua Bishop performs in his first Test cap, and the result of the two-match series against Pakistan, who arrive off a dominant warm-up win.

key_terms.definition: A player's first-ever selection to a national squad for a Test match, cricket's longest and most prestigious format.
A bowler who delivers the ball with their left hand, spinning it away from a right-handed batter — a specific and valued skill in cricket attacks.

stories.the_lesson: Rebuilding a national team isn't just about winning the next match — it's also about deliberately creating new opportunities, like debuting fresh talent and opening new venues, that pay off over years rather than one series.

stories.title: Ruturaj Gaikwad named West Zone captain for the Duleep Trophy, leading a squad with Prithvi Shaw's comeback
key_points: Gaikwad captains, Mumbai's Shams Mulani is vice-captain
Prithvi Shaw picked after a strong season for Maharashtra
Squad also includes Sarfaraz Khan, Musheer Khan, Shardul Thakur
Duleep Trophy runs August 23 to September 10 in Bengaluru
stories.background: The Duleep Trophy is one of Indian domestic cricket's oldest tournaments, pitting regional 'zone' teams (like West Zone, North Zone) against each other — historically a proving ground where players make the case for national selection. Prithvi Shaw, once seen as a future India opener, has struggled to nail down a regular spot in recent years, so his inclusion here is being read as a chance to rebuild momentum.
stories.what_happened: India's West Zone selection committee named Ruturaj Gaikwad — who also captains the Chennai Super Kings in the IPL — as captain for the 2026-27 Duleep Trophy, with Mumbai all-rounder Shams Mulani as his deputy. The squad includes Prithvi Shaw, Sarfaraz Khan, Musheer Khan and Shardul Thakur among other notable names. The tournament runs from August 23 to September 10 at the BCCI Centre of Excellence in Bengaluru.
stories.why_it_matters: Zonal domestic tournaments like the Duleep Trophy are where India's national selectors watch players perform against strong regional opposition outside the IPL bubble — a good showing here can be the difference between a player getting a national call-up or fading out. Shaw's return to a marquee squad after a rocky few years makes this a genuine watch-point for his career, not just a routine squad announcement.
stories.ripple_effects: Strong Duleep Trophy performances from players like Shaw, Musheer Khan or Sarfaraz Khan could put pressure on India's existing Test and white-ball squads ahead of the busier part of the domestic season. It also keeps Gaikwad's leadership profile growing outside the IPL, which matters if he's ever considered for national captaincy roles down the line.
stories.why_now: West Zone's selectors announced the squad this week as domestic teams finalize their line-ups ahead of the tournament's August 23 start.
stories.watch_next: Watch how Prithvi Shaw actually performs once the tournament starts on August 23 — this squad selection is only the opportunity, not the outcome.

key_terms.definition: An Indian first-class cricket tournament contested between regional 'zone' teams, used partly as a selection trial for the national side.
India's states are grouped into regional zones (East, West, North, South, Central) for certain tournaments, mixing players from multiple state teams onto one side.

stories.the_lesson: In sports, a 'comeback' usually isn't a single dramatic moment — it's getting picked for the right domestic stage where good performances are actually seen by the people who make national selection calls.

stories.title: Oval Invincibles are now 'MI London' — and the Mumbai Indians-owned team won its first match under the new name
key_points: Reliance's Mumbai Indians group bought 49% of Oval Invincibles for £123m
Team renamed MI London, switched to Mumbai Indians' blue and gold
MI London beat Sunrisers Leeds by 7 wickets in the season opener
The Hundred now has two 'London' teams — MI London and London Spirit
stories.background: The Hundred is English cricket's short-format competition (100 balls a side), and in 2025 its eight teams sold minority stakes to outside investors — including several IPL franchise owners — to raise money and boost the tournament's global profile. Reliance, the Indian conglomerate that owns the IPL's Mumbai Indians, bought a 49% stake in the Oval Invincibles as part of that sale.
stories.what_happened: Ahead of the 2026 season, Oval Invincibles rebranded entirely as MI London, adopting Mumbai Indians' blue-and-gold colors in place of the team's old green. In its first match under the new identity, MI London beat Sunrisers Leeds by 7 wickets to open the 2026 season. Confusingly, the Hundred now has two separate 'London' teams, since the Lord's-based London Spirit — majority-owned by an American investment group, Tech Titans — did not rebrand and kept its own name.
stories.why_it_matters: This is IPL money and branding visibly reshaping English domestic cricket — the same ownership group behind one of the IPL's biggest franchises is now running a team in a rival country's league, using the same colors and name. It's a preview of what cricket's ownership landscape may look like as IPL franchises increasingly buy into leagues abroad, blurring the line between separate national competitions.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect more cross-branding as other IPL owners with stakes in Hundred teams consider similar renames, and expect other global T20 leagues to watch how fans react to a historic English ground's team taking on an Indian franchise identity. It also raises the profile — and likely the broadcast value — of MI London's matches in the huge Indian market, which is exactly the commercial logic behind the investment.
stories.why_now: The Hundred's 2026 season began this week (matches from July 21), making this MI London's first competitive outing under its new identity and ownership.
stories.watch_next: Watch whether MI London's early form and its Indian-market pull start to reshape which Hundred matches get the most attention and investment this season.

key_terms.definition: An English T20-style cricket tournament where each side bowls 100 balls (instead of the usual 120), designed to be shorter and more TV-friendly.
Owning less than half (here, 49%) of a company or team — enough for major influence like a rebrand, but not full control.

stories.the_lesson: Sports leagues increasingly compete for the same global investors and audiences — when a foreign league's biggest brand buys into your local team, it's a sign the boundaries between 'domestic' and 'international' sport are dissolving.

stories.title: Nepal's World Cup qualifying hopes take a hit as Netherlands chase them down with 35 balls to spare
key_points: Netherlands beat Nepal by 6 wickets in the ICC Cricket World Cup League 2 in Utrecht
Nepal were bowled out for 157; Netherlands chased it down with overs to spare
Wicketkeeper-captain Scott Edwards top-scored with 61 not out and won Player of the Match
The loss makes it harder for Nepal to break into League 2's automatic-qualifying top 4
stories.background: The ICC Cricket World Cup League 2 is a multi-year one-day-international competition for lower-ranked cricket nations like Nepal, Netherlands, Namibia, Oman and the USA, where the top four finishers earn a route toward qualifying for the 50-over Cricket World Cup. Nepal went into today's match sitting fifth in the table with 28 points from 28 games — just outside that top-four cutoff — as part of a tri-series with Netherlands and Namibia running through July 31.
stories.what_happened: Netherlands beat Nepal by 6 wickets in Utrecht on Thursday, bowling Nepal out for 157 before chasing the target down with 35 balls unused. Netherlands captain and wicketkeeper Scott Edwards anchored the chase with an unbeaten 61 off 89 balls and was named Player of the Match.
stories.why_it_matters: Every League 2 match is close to must-win for a team like Nepal — missing the top four sends them down a lower-probability path to World Cup qualification, or out of the picture altogether. These matches get a fraction of the attention India or England fixtures do, but they decide which nations even get a shot at cricket's biggest stage.
stories.ripple_effects: The loss makes Nepal's path back into the automatic-qualifying top four steeper with fewer League 2 matches left to play, while Netherlands strengthen their own position.
